SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with a well-specified formulation (Sec.2, Alg.1) and extensive empirical evaluation on the spaceship tasks (Sec.3). The mathematical formulation of the meta-level MDP and the training procedures (Appendix A.1, B) are sound and internally consistent; using a differentiable critic to backpropagate to the controller is a reasonable practical solution. Experimental methodology includes sensible baselines (reactive, iterative), multiple experts, and thorough hyperparameter sweeps (Sec.3, D.2), but lacks statistical significance reporting and some training instability is acknowledged (D.3). The paper also admits and demonstrates limitations (entropy regularizer producing suboptimal nondeterministic manager policies in Sec.3.3 and D.3), so while conclusions are generally justified by the results, a few practical caveats and missing statistical analyses prevent a perfect score.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable new formulation—a learned metacontroller that jointly decides how many internal model-based ‘imagination’ iterations to run and which expert model to consult, recasting the speed–accuracy tradeoff as an RL problem (Eq. 3, Sec. 2.2 and A.1). While it builds on established ideas (bounded-rational metareasoning, adaptive computation time, learned optimizers), the integration of a manager policy πM that selects experts of differing cost/reliability and stopping decisions is a non-trivial and clearly articulated extension (manager and expert selection described in Sec. 2.2, Sec. 2.3, and experiments). The approach and experiments (using INs as experts to solve complex dynamics) would be of real interest to experts, but do not constitute a radical departure from prior lines of work—hence a strong-but-not-maximal novelty score.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro → Model → Experiments → Discussion) and clearly states its core contribution early (a metacontroller that adaptively chooses experts and number of ponder steps, see Intro and Sec. 2). Notation for losses, histories, experts and the manager is introduced and used consistently (Sec. 2 and Appendix A), and the inclusion of Algorithms 1–3 and detailed experiment descriptions (Sec. 3, Appendix C–D) makes the methodology and spacecraft examples easy to follow. Presentation issues are minor: several long sentences and a few small formatting/repetition artifacts near the end (duplicate paragraphs/’TeX’ leftover) slightly detract from readability but do not obscure the main ideas.
